q: A motor operating on 100 V draws a current of 1 A. If the efficiency of the motor is $91.6 \%$, then the loss of power in units of $\mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{s}$ is
question: A motor operating on 100 V draws a current of 1 A. If the efficiency of the motor is $91.6 \%$, then the loss of power in units of $\mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{s}$ is
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & P_{\text {input }}=V i=100 \mathrm{~W} \\ & \eta=\frac{P_{\text {out }}}{P_{\text {input }}}=0.916\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & P_{\text {out }}=91.6 \mathrm{~W} \\ & \text { Loss }=100-91.6=8.4 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{s}=2 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

question: In a meter bridge as shown in the figure, it is given that resistance Y=12.5 Ω  and that the balance is obtained at a distance 39.5 cm from end A (by jockey J). After interchanging the resistances X and Y a new balance point is found at a distance l2 from end A. What are the values of X (in Ω) and l2?
solution:
For a balanced meter bridge, (y)×​39.5=x×100-39.5, 

x=12.5×39.560.5=8.16 Ω. When x and y are interchanged l 1 and ( 100− l 1 ) will also interchange, l2=60.5 cm.

subject: Physics
q: Two resistors of $200 \, \Omega$ and $400 \, \Omega$ are connected in series with a battery of $100$ V. A bulb rated at $200$ V, $100$ W is connected across the $400 \, \Omega$ resistance. The potential drop across the bulb is _______ V.
question: Two resistors of $200 \, \Omega$ and $400 \, \Omega$ are connected in series with a battery of $100$ V. A bulb rated at $200$ V, $100$ W is connected across the $400 \, \Omega$ resistance. The potential drop across the bulb is _______ V.
solution:
The resistance of the bulb is calculated using its power rating:

$R_b = \dfrac{V^2}{P} = \dfrac{200^2}{100} = 400 \, \Omega$

The bulb is connected in parallel with the $400 \, \Omega$ resistor. The equivalent resistance of this parallel combination is:

$R_p = \dfrac{400 \times 400}{400 + 400} = 200 \, \Omega$

This parallel combination is in series with the $200 \, \Omega$ resistor. The total equivalent resistance of the circuit is:

$R_{eq} = 200 + R_p = 200 + 200 = 400 \, \Omega$

The current drawn from the battery is:

$I = \dfrac{V}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{100}{400} = 0.25 \text{ A}$

The potential drop across the bulb is the same as the potential drop across the parallel combination:

$V_p = I \times R_p = 0.25 \times 200 = 50 \text{ V}$

Answer: $50$

q: Two identical cells each of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> are connected in parallel across a parallel combination of two resistors each of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. A voltmeter connected in the circuit measures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The internal resistance of each cell is :
question: Two identical cells each of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> are connected in parallel across a parallel combination of two resistors each of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. A voltmeter connected in the circuit measures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The internal resistance of each cell is :
solution:
Equivalent resistance of the parallel 20 Ω branches will be 10 Ω.

Equivalent EMF of the two branches which are in parallel will be, =1.5r+1.5r1r+1r=1.5 V and the equivalent internal resistance for same branches will be, r2.

For loop containing voltmeter and 10 Ω resistor, 1.2-10i=0 ⇒i=0.12 A.

Reading of voltmeter should be,

1.2=1.5-ir2 ⇒ir2=0.3 ⇒r=0.6i=0.60.12= 5 Ω

subject: Physics
q: If the potential difference between B and D is zero, the value of x is 1n Ω. The value of n is ______.
question: If the potential difference between B and D is zero, the value of x is 1n Ω. The value of n is ______.
solution:
As potential difference between B & D is zero therefore it is a balanced wheatstone bridge. So, we can write

⇒R1R2=R3R4⇒6×36+31+2=x×1x+1x

⇒23=1x+1

⇒x=0.5=12

Hence, required n=2.

subject: Physics
q: When an external resistance of $5\ \Omega$ is connected across terminals of a cell, a current of $0.25\text{ A}$ flows through it. When the $5\ \Omega$ resistor is replaced by a $2\ \Omega$ resistor, a current of $0.5\text{ A}$ flows through it. The internal resistance of the cell is _______ $\Omega$.
question: When an external resistance of $5\ \Omega$ is connected across terminals of a cell, a current of $0.25\text{ A}$ flows through it. When the $5\ \Omega$ resistor is replaced by a $2\ \Omega$ resistor, a current of $0.5\text{ A}$ flows through it. The internal resistance of the cell is _______ $\Omega$.
solution:
Let the EMF of the cell be $E$ and its internal resistance be $r$.

The current $I$ in the circuit is given by $I = \dfrac{E}{R + r}$.

For the first case, $R_1 = 5\ \Omega$ and $I_1 = 0.25\text{ A}$:

$0.25 = \dfrac{E}{5 + r} \Rightarrow E = 0.25(5 + r)$

For the second case, $R_2 = 2\ \Omega$ and $I_2 = 0.5\text{ A}$:

$0.5 = \dfrac{E}{2 + r} \Rightarrow E = 0.5(2 + r)$

Equating the expressions for $E$:

$0.25(5 + r) = 0.5(2 + r)$

$5 + r = 2(2 + r)$

$5 + r = 4 + 2r$

$r = 1\ \Omega$

Answer: $1$

q: A uniform metallic wire carries a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery is connected across it. The mass of uniform metallic wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and resistivity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>  The length of wire is :
question: A uniform metallic wire carries a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery is connected across it. The mass of uniform metallic wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and resistivity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>  The length of wire is :
solution:
Given here, I=2 A, V=3.4 V, m=8.92×10-3 kg,

d=8.92×103 kg m-3 and ρ=1.7×10-8 Ω-m

From Ohm's law, resistance is R=VI=3.42=1.7 Ω

As resistance of wire is given by R=ρlA, where, A is area and l is length of wire.

 ⇒1.7=ρlA ⇒l=1.7Aρ

Now, volume=md=8.92×10-38.92×103=10-6 m3

Thus, 

l2=1.7Aρ×l=1.7volumelρ×l=1.7ρ10-6=1.71.7×10-8×10-6

Hence, l=10 m

subject: Physics
q: Current passing through a wire as function of time is given as $\mathrm{I}(\mathrm{t})=0.02 \mathrm{t}+0.01 \mathrm{~A}$. The charge that will flow through the wire from $t=1 s$ to $t=2 s$ is :
question: Current passing through a wire as function of time is given as $\mathrm{I}(\mathrm{t})=0.02 \mathrm{t}+0.01 \mathrm{~A}$. The charge that will flow through the wire from $t=1 s$ to $t=2 s$ is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{q}=\int \mathrm{idt} \\ & \int_0^2(0.02 \mathrm{t}+0.01) \mathrm{dt}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{q}=\left[0.02 \frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{2}+0.01 \mathrm{t}\right]_1^2 \\ & =0.01(3)+0.01(1) \\ & =0.04 \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

question: The length of a wire of a potentiometer is $100 \mathrm{~cm}$, and the e.m.f. of its standard cell is $\mathrm{E}$ volt. solution:
Potential $\propto \mathrm{R}$
$\mathrm{R} \propto$ length $\Rightarrow$ Potential difference $\propto l$

q: Ratio of thermal energy released in two resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math> connected in parallel in an electric circuit is :
question: Ratio of thermal energy released in two resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math> connected in parallel in an electric circuit is :
solution:
In parallel connection, potential difference across resistors remains the same.

Thermal energy released in resistor is given by H=V2R×t

So, the ratio is H1H2=V2tRV2t3R=3RR=3:1.

subject: Physics
q: In the experiment of Ohm's law, a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is applied across the end of a conductor of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and diameter of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The measured current in the conductor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum permissible percentage error in the resistivity of the conductor is :-
question: In the experiment of Ohm's law, a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is applied across the end of a conductor of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and diameter of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The measured current in the conductor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum permissible percentage error in the resistivity of the conductor is :-
solution:
R=ρℓA=VI
ρ=AVIℓ=πd2 V4Iℓ  A=πd24
∴  Δρρ=2Δdd+ΔVV+ΔII+Δℓℓ
Δρρ=20.015.00+0.15.0+0.012.00+0.110.0
Δρρ=0.004+0.02+0.005+0.01
Δρρ=0.039
% error =Δρρ×100=0.039×100=3.90%.

question: The ratio of heat dissipated per second through the resistance $5 \Omega$ and $10 \Omega$ in the circuit given below is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{i}_1}{\mathrm{i}_2}=\frac{10}{5}=\frac{2}{1} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_1}{\mathrm{P}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{i}_1^2 \mathrm{R}_1}{\mathrm{i}_2^2 \mathrm{R}_2}=\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)^2 \times \frac{5}{10}=\frac{2}{1}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure the voltmeter reads 2 V across 5 Ω resistor. The resistance of the voltmeter is _____ Ω
question: As shown in the figure the voltmeter reads 2 V across 5 Ω resistor. The resistance of the voltmeter is _____ Ω
solution:
Let the resistance of the voltmeter be R Ω. The voltmeter is in parallel with the 5 Ω. So the total resistance in the circuit is 

5R5+R+2 Ω

Applying Ohm's law in the whole circuit, V=IR,

I=3 2+5R5+R A

It is given that the potential difference across 5 Ω is 2 V.

Thus, by Ohm's law,

32+5R5+R×5RR+5=2⇒15+3R10+7R×5R5+R=2⇒15R=20+14R⇒R=20 Ω

subject: Physics
q: Two cells are connected in opposition as shown. Cell E1 is of 8 V emf and 2 Ω internal resistance; the cell E2 is of 2 V emf and 4 Ω internal resistance. The terminal potential difference of cell E2 is ______ V.
question: Two cells are connected in opposition as shown. Cell E1 is of 8 V emf and 2 Ω internal resistance; the cell E2 is of 2 V emf and 4 Ω internal resistance. The terminal potential difference of cell E2 is ______ V.
solution:
Current in circuit, I=8-22+4=66=1 A

Applying Kirchhoff's law from C to B,

VC-2-4×1=VB

⇒VC-VB=6 V

The internal resistance of the cell is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>.
solution:
Let potential gradient be x.

For the first case, we can write

εr+5×5=200x ......(1)

and after adding resistance, we can write

εr+15×15=300x .....(2)

From both equations, we get

⇒r=5 Ω

f= 1 2

question: A piece of copper and another of germanium are cooled from room temperature to $77 \mathrm{~K}$, the resistance of

q: To measure the temperature coefficient of resistivity α of a semiconductor, an electrical arrangement shown in the figure is prepared. The arm BC is made up of the semiconductor. The experiment is being conducted at 25°C and resistance of the semiconductor arm is 3 mΩ. Arm BC is cooled at a constant rate of 2 °C s-1. If the galvanometer G shows no deflection after 10 s, then α is:
question: To measure the temperature coefficient of resistivity α of a semiconductor, an electrical arrangement shown in the figure is prepared. The arm BC is made up of the semiconductor. The experiment is being conducted at 25°C and resistance of the semiconductor arm is 3 mΩ. Arm BC is cooled at a constant rate of 2 °C s-1. If the galvanometer G shows no deflection after 10 s, then α is:
solution:
For no deflection(balanced condition) 0.81=R3

⇒R=2.4 mΩ

Temperature fall in 10 s = 20 °C

ΔR=RαΔt

⇒α=ΔRRΔt=2.4-33×20

=-1×10-2 °C-1

q: In an experiment to find emf of a cell using potentiometer, the length of null point for a cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If this cell is replaced by another cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>. the length-of null point increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. question: In an experiment to find emf of a cell using potentiometer, the length of null point for a cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If this cell is replaced by another cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>. the length-of null point increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
We know, the condition where the galvanometer shows a null point is,

E1E2=l1l2

So, 1.5E2=6060+40=610=35

⇒E2=52 V

Hence, the value of x=25.

subject: Physics
q: For the two cells having same EMF $E$ and internal resistance $r$, the current passing through the external resistor $6 \Omega$ is same when both the cells are connected either in parallel or in series. The value of internal resistance $r$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
question: For the two cells having same EMF $E$ and internal resistance $r$, the current passing through the external resistor $6 \Omega$ is same when both the cells are connected either in parallel or in series. The value of internal resistance $r$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
solution:
For series connection of two cells: $I_s = \frac{2E}{2r + 6}$.
For parallel connection: $I_p = \frac{E}{r/2 + 6} = \frac{2E}{r + 12}$.
Given $I_s = I_p$: $\frac{2E}{2r + 6} = \frac{2E}{r + 12}$.
This gives $2r + 6 = r + 12$, so $r = 6\Omega$.
